questions.question: For election to the Lok Sabha, a nomination paper can be filed by
questions.option_2: a resident of the constituency from which the election is to be contested.
questions.option_3: any citizen of India whose name appears in the electoral roll of a constituency.
questions.solution:
According to the Representation of the People Act, 1951, the eligibility for filing a nomination paper for the Lok Sabha is as follows:

  • Citizenship: The candidate must be a citizen of India.
  • Electoral Roll: The candidate's name must be registered in the electoral roll of any parliamentary constituency in the country.
  • Constituency Flexibility: A person does not need to be a resident of the specific constituency from which they are contesting. For example, a person registered to vote in Delhi can contest an election from a constituency in Uttar Pradesh.

questions.question:
Consider the following statements:

  1. In the election for Lok Sabha or State Assembly, the winning candidate must get at least 50 percent of the votes polled, to be declared elected.
  2. According to the provisions laid down in the Constitution of India in Lok Sabha, the Speaker's post goes to the majority party and the Deputy Speaker's to the Opposition.

Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.solution:
The electoral and procedural systems in India follow these principles:

  • First-Past-The-Post (FPTP): In Lok Sabha and State Assembly elections, India follows the FPTP system. A candidate is declared elected if they receive more votes than any other candidate, even if they do not secure a majority (50% + 1) of the total votes polled.
  • Speaker and Deputy Speaker: The Constitution does not mandate which party the Speaker or Deputy Speaker must belong to. While Article 93 provides for their election, the practice of the Speaker coming from the majority party and the Deputy Speaker from the Opposition is a parliamentary convention established since the 11th Lok Sabha, not a constitutional provision.

questions.question: The Parliament of India acquires the power to legislate on any item in the State List in the national interest if a resolution to that effect is passed by the
questions.option_1: Lok Sabha by a simple majority of its total membership
questions.option_2: Lok Sabha by a majority of not less than two-thirds of its total membership
questions.option_3: Rajya Sabha by a simple majority of its total membership
questions.option_4: Rajya Sabha by a majority of not less than two-thirds of its members present and voting
questions.solution: Under Article 249 of the Constitution, the Parliament can legislate on subjects in the State List if the Rajya Sabha passes a resolution supported by not less than two-thirds of the members present and voting. Such a resolution remains in force for a period not exceeding one year at a time, but can be renewed indefinitely.

questions.question:
Which of the following statements is/are correct?

  1. A bill pending in the Lok Sabha lapses on its prorogation.
  2. A Bill pending in the Rajya Sabha, which has not been passed by the Lok Sabha, shall not lapse on the dissolution of the Lok Sabha.

Select the correct answer using the code below:
questions.solution:
The rules regarding the 'lapsing' of bills are critical in Indian parliamentary procedure:

  • Prorogation vs. Dissolution: Prorogation only ends a session, not the life of the House. Therefore, a bill pending in the Lok Sabha does not lapse on prorogation; it is carried over to the next session.
  • Rajya Sabha Bills: A bill that originated in and is pending in the Rajya Sabha, and has not yet been passed by the Lok Sabha, does not lapse upon the dissolution of the Lok Sabha because the Rajya Sabha is a permanent body.

questions.question:
Consider the following statements:

  1. The Legislative Council of a state in India can be larger in size than half of the Legislative Assembly of that particular state.
  2. The Governor of a state nominates the Chairman of the Legislative Council of that particular state.

Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.solution:
The composition of the State Legislative Council (Vidhan Parishad) is governed by Article 171:

  • Size Limit: The maximum strength of the Council is fixed at one-third of the total strength of the Legislative Assembly of that state, and the minimum strength is fixed at 40.
  • Chairman: The Chairman of the Legislative Council is elected by the members of the Council from among themselves, not nominated by the Governor.

questions.question: When a bill is referred to a joint sitting of both the Houses of the Parliament, it has to be passed by
questions.option_1: a simple majority of members present and voting
questions.option_2: three-fourths majority of members present and voting
questions.solution: A joint sitting of Parliament (Article 108) is presided over by the Speaker of the Lok Sabha. At this sitting, the bill is passed by a simple majority of the total number of members of both Houses present and voting. Since the Lok Sabha has a significantly larger membership than the Rajya Sabha, its will usually prevails in a joint sitting.

questions.question: Which one of the following is the largest Committee of the Parliament?
questions.solution:
Parliamentary committees vary in size and composition:

CommitteeTotal MembersComposition
Estimates Committee30All 30 from Lok Sabha
Public Accounts Committee2215 (LS) + 7 (RS)
Public Undertakings Committee2215 (LS) + 7 (RS)
Committee on Petitions2515 (LS) + 10 (RS)

The Estimates Committee is the largest committee of the Parliament.

questions.question: What will follow if a Money Bill is substantially amended by the Rajya Sabha?
questions.option_1: The Lok Sabha may still proceed with the Bill, accepting or not accepting the recommendations of the Rajya Sabha
questions.option_2: The Lok Sabha cannot consider the Bill further
questions.option_3: The Lok Sabha may send the Bill to the Rajya Sabha for reconsideration
questions.option_4: The President may call a joint sitting for passing the Bill
questions.solution:
If the Rajya Sabha suggests substantial amendments to a Money Bill, the Lok Sabha has supreme authority under Article 109. The Lok Sabha may:

  • Accept all recommendations and pass the bill in the amended form.
  • Reject all recommendations and pass the bill in its original form.
  • Accept some and reject others.

In all these cases, the bill is deemed to have been passed by both Houses. The Rajya Sabha cannot block or reconsider a Money Bill beyond its 14-day window.

questions.question:
Consider the following statements:

  1. The Chairman and the Deputy Chairman of the Rajya Sabha are not the members of that House.
  2. While the nominated members of the two Houses of the Parliament have no voting right in the presidential election, they have the right to vote in the election of the Vice President.

Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.solution:
Membership and voting rights in the Rajya Sabha and for the Vice President:

  • Chairman: The Vice President of India is the ex-officio Chairman of the Rajya Sabha and is not a member of the House. However, the Deputy Chairman is elected from among the members of the Rajya Sabha, so he/she is a member.
  • Vice-Presidential Election: The electoral college for the Vice President consists of all members of both Houses of Parliament (Elected + Nominated). In contrast, nominated members cannot vote in the Presidential election.

questions.question: Which of the following special powers have been conferred on the Rajya Sabha by the Constitution of India?
questions.option_1: To change the existing territory of a State and to change the name of a State
questions.option_2: To pass a resolution empowering the Parliament to make laws in the State List and to create one or more All India Services
questions.option_3: To amend the election procedure of the President and to determine the pension of the President after his/her retirement
questions.option_4: To determine the functions of the Election Commission and to determine the number of Election Commissioners
questions.solution:
The Rajya Sabha enjoys two major exclusive/special powers under the Constitution:

  1. Article 249: Authorizing Parliament to make laws on a subject in the State List in the national interest.
  2. Article 312: Authorizing the creation of one or more All India Services (common to Union and States).

questions.question:
Which of the following are the methods of Parliamentary control over public finance in India?

  1. Placing Annual Financial Statement before the Parliament
  2. Withdrawal of money from Consolidated Fund of India only after passing the Appropriation Bill
  3. Provisions of supplementary grants and vote-on-account
  4. Aperiodic or at least a mid-year review of programme of the Government against macroeconomic forecasts and expenditure by a Parliamentary Budget Office
  5. Introducing Finance Bill in the Parliament

Select the correct answer using the codes given below:
questions.solution:
Parliamentary control over public finance is exercised through several stages and instruments:

  • Annual Financial Statement: Budget presentation (Article 112).
  • Appropriation Bill: No money can be withdrawn from the Consolidated Fund without this legal authorization (Article 114).
  • Supplementary Grants: For additional expenditure (Article 115) and Vote-on-account for short-term spending (Article 116).
  • Finance Bill: For the taxation/revenue side of the budget.

Note: There is currently no statutory "Parliamentary Budget Office" in India that conducts aperiodic reviews against macroeconomic forecasts in the manner described.

questions.question: In the Parliament of India, the purpose of an adjournment motion is:
questions.option_1: To allow a discussion on a definite matter of urgent public importance
questions.option_2: To let opposition collect information from the ministers
questions.option_3: To allow a reduction of specific amount in demand for grant
questions.option_4: To postpone the proceedings to check the inappropriate or violent behavior on the part of some members
questions.solution: An Adjournment Motion is an extraordinary device used in the Lok Sabha to draw the attention of the House to a definite matter of urgent public importance. It interrupts the regular business of the House and involves an element of censure against the government.

questions.question:
Consider the following statements:

  1. Union Territories are not represented in the Rajya Sabha.
  2. It is within the preview of the Chief Election Commissioner to adjudicate election disputes.
  3. According to the Constitution of India, the Parliament consists of the Lok Sabha and the Rajya Sabha only.

Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.solution:
Clarifying misconceptions about the Parliament and Elections:

  • UT Representation: Union Territories are represented in the Rajya Sabha (e.g., Delhi, Puducherry, and Jammu & Kashmir).
  • Election Disputes: The High Courts have the jurisdiction to adjudicate election disputes (Article 329). The Election Commission handles the conduct of elections but not the judicial adjudication of disputes.
  • Composition of Parliament: Under Article 79, Parliament consists of the President, the Lok Sabha, and the Rajya Sabha.

questions.question:
Regarding the office of the Lok Sabha Speaker, consider the following statements:

  1. He/She holds the office during the pleasure of the President.
  2. He/She need not be a member of the House at the time of his/her election but has to become a member of the House within six months from the date of his/her election.
  3. If he/she intends to resign, the letter of his/her resignation has to be addressed to the Deputy Speaker.

Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.solution:
The Office of the Speaker has specific legal protections:

  • Tenure: The Speaker does not hold office during the pleasure of the President. He/She is elected by the House and can only be removed by a resolution passed by a majority of all the then members of the Lok Sabha.
  • Membership: The Speaker must be a member of the Lok Sabha at the time of election. If he/she ceases to be a member, the office must be vacated.
  • Resignation: The Speaker submits his/her resignation to the Deputy Speaker (and vice versa).

questions.question: The authorization for the withdrawal of funds from the Consolidated Fund of India must come from
questions.solution: No money can be withdrawn from the Consolidated Fund of India except under appropriation made by law. This 'authorization' must come from the Parliament through the passing of the Appropriation Bill (Article 114).

questions.question: All revenues received by the Union Government by way of taxes and other receipts for the conduct of Government business are credited to the
questions.solution: Article 266 defines the Consolidated Fund of India. All revenues received by the Government of India, all loans raised by the Government, and all money received in repayment of loans form part of this fund.

questions.question: When the annual Union Budget is not passed by the Lok Sabha,
questions.option_1: the Budget is modified and presented again
questions.option_2: the Budget is referred to the Rajya Sabha for suggestions
questions.option_3: the Union Finance Minister is asked to resign
questions.option_4: the Prime Minister submits the resignation of Council of Ministers
questions.solution: The Union Budget includes the Demands for Grants and the Annual Financial Statement. Since the Council of Ministers is collectively responsible to the Lok Sabha (Article 75), the failure to pass the Budget implies a lack of confidence in the government. Consequently, the Prime Minister must submit the resignation of the entire Council of Ministers.

questions.question:
What is the difference between "vote-on-account" and "interim budget"?

  1. The provision of a "vote-on-account" is used by a regular Government, while an "interim budget" is a provision used by a caretaker Government.
  2. A "vote-on-account" only deals with the expenditure in the Government's budget, while an "interim budget" includes both expenditure and receipts.

Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.solution:
Distinguishing between two budgetary terms:

  • Scope: A "vote-on-account" only deals with the expenditure side (allowing the government to function until the full budget is passed). An "interim budget" is a complete set of accounts including both expenditure and receipts.
  • Usage: Both can be used by a regular government, particularly in an election year, though an interim budget is more comprehensive.

questions.question: Which one of the following is responsible for the preparation and presentation of the Union Budget to the Parliament?
questions.solution: The Department of Economic Affairs, under the Ministry of Finance, is the nodal agency responsible for preparing and presenting the Union Budget to the Parliament.

questions.question:
Consider the following statements: The Constitution of India provides that

  1. the Legislative Assembly of each State shall consist of not more than 450 members chosen by direct election from territorial constituencies in the State.
  2. a person shall not be qualified to be chosen to fill a seat in the Legislative Assembly of a State if he/she is less than 25 years of age.

Which of the statements above is/are correct?
questions.solution:
Provisions for the State Legislative Assembly under Article 170:

  • Maximum Strength: The Assembly shall consist of not more than 500 members (not 450) and not less than 60 members.
  • Age Qualification: Article 173 specifies that a person must be at least 25 years of age to be a member of the Legislative Assembly.

questions.question:
Consider the following statements:

  1. The Rajya Sabha alone has the power to declare that it would be in national interest for the Parliament to legislate with respect to a matter in the State List.
  2. Resolutions approving the Proclamation of Emergency are passed only by the Lok Sabha.

Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.solution:
Comparing the powers of the Houses:

  • State List Legislation: Only the Rajya Sabha can initiate a resolution under Article 249 to allow Parliament to legislate on State List subjects.
  • Emergency Proclamation: A proclamation of Emergency must be approved by both Houses of Parliament within a specified timeframe (one month for National Emergency).

questions.question: With reference to the Indian Parliament, which one of the following is not correct?
questions.option_1: The Appropriation Bill must be passed by both the Houses of Parliament before it can be enacted into law.
questions.option_2: No money shall be withdrawn from the Consolidated Fund of India except under the appropriation made by the Appropriation Act.
questions.option_3: Finance Bill is required for proposing new taxes but no another Bill/Act is required for making changes in the rates of taxes which are already under operation.
questions.option_4: No Money Bill can be introduced except on the recommendation of the President.
questions.solution:
Regarding financial procedures in Parliament:

  • Appropriation Bill: It must be passed by both Houses (though Rajya Sabha's powers are limited similar to a Money Bill) before it becomes an Act.
  • Finance Bill: According to the Provisional Collection of Taxes Act, 1931, a Finance Bill is required not just for new taxes but also for altering or continuing existing tax rates. Statement 3 in the question is incorrect because changes in rates do require legislative approval via the Finance Bill.
  • Presidential Recommendation: Money Bills require prior recommendation under Article 117(1).

questions.question: Which one of the following statements is not correct?
questions.option_1: In Lok Sabha, a no-confidence motion has to set out the grounds on which it is based.
questions.option_2: In the case of a no-confidence motion in Lok Sabha, no conditions of admissibility have been laid down in the Rules.
questions.option_3: A motion of no-confidence once admitted, has to be taken up within ten days of the leave being granted.
questions.option_4: Rajya Sabha is not empowered to entertain a motion of no-confidence.
questions.solution:
Rules regarding the No-Confidence Motion in the Lok Sabha (Rule 198):

  • Grounds: The motion does not need to state the grounds on which it is based. It simply states that 'This House expresses its want of confidence in the Council of Ministers.'
  • Admissibility: The only condition is that it must be supported by at least 50 members.
  • Timeline: Once admitted, the Speaker specifies a date for discussion within 10 days.
  • Rajya Sabha: Since the government is only responsible to the Lok Sabha, the Rajya Sabha cannot entertain this motion.

questions.question:
Consider the following statements:

  1. The Speaker of Lok Sabha has the power to adjourn the House sine die but, on prorogation, it is only the President who can summon the House.
  2. Unless sooner dissolved or there is an extension of the term, there is an automatic dissolution of the Lok Sabha by efflux of time, at the end of the period of five years, even if no formal order of dissolution is issued by the President.
  3. The Speaker of Lok Sabha continues in office even after the dissolution of the House and until immediately before the first meeting of the House.

Which of these statements given above are correct?
questions.solution:
Mechanisms of the Lok Sabha:

  • Adjournment vs Prorogation: The Speaker adjourns the House (temporary suspension), but only the President can prorogue (end the session) or summon the House.
  • Automatic Dissolution: The Lok Sabha expires automatically after five years from the date of its first meeting, unless dissolved earlier by the President. No formal order is strictly required for this 'efflux of time' dissolution.
  • Speaker's Tenure: The Speaker does not vacate office on dissolution. He/She continues until the new Lok Sabha meets.

questions.question:
Consider the following statements:

  1. While members of the Rajya Sabha are associated with Committees on Public Accounts and Public Undertakings, members of Committee on Estimates are drawn entirely from Lok Sabha.
  2. The Ministry of Parliamentary Affairs works under the overall direction of Cabinet Committee on Parliamentary Affairs.
  3. The Minister of Parliamentary Affairs nominates Members of Parliament on Committees, Councils, Boards and Commissions etc. set up by the Government of India in the various ministries.

Which of these statements is/are correct?
questions.solution:
Parliamentary Committees and Ministry roles:

  • Estimates Committee: Unique because all its 30 members are from the Lok Sabha. Public Accounts and Public Undertakings committees have members from both Houses.
  • Ministry of Parliamentary Affairs: Handles the management of government business in Parliament and acts under the Cabinet Committee on Parliamentary Affairs.
  • Nominations: The Minister of Parliamentary Affairs is responsible for nominating MPs to various statutory and non-statutory bodies set up by the Government.
